title: Best Man For The Gob

jokes.text: Michael meeting with the company accountant named 'Ira Gilligan'
jokes.setup: The accountant is introduced formally by the narrator as 'Ira Gilligan'
jokes.punchline: The name is a double reference: Gilligan's Island's hapless first mate, plus 'IRA' (Individual Retirement Account) — layered wordplay that pays off repeatedly throughout the episode
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in Arrested Development naming conventions. 'Ira Gilligan' simultaneously evokes the incompetent Gilligan (fitting for someone unwittingly entangled in the Bluths' schemes) and sets up the IRA financial pun that George Sr. delivers later. The joke earns its high craft score because it operates on multiple levels simultaneously and rewards attentive viewers. The rewatch bonus is significant — on first viewing it seems like a quirky name; on rewatch the IRA joke lands even harder.

jokes.text: The thermostat gag: Michael's office is freezing while George Sr.'s office (via satellite/adjacent) is hot — Ira keeps commenting on the temperature instead of the financial crisis
jokes.setup: Ira is mid-sentence explaining a massive financial discrepancy ('there's basically no money where it should be')
jokes.punchline: He keeps breaking off to complain about the temperature rather than the catastrophic financial news he's delivering
jokes.explanation: The absurdist escalation of temperature complaints interrupting devastating financial news is well-structured. George Sr. controlling the thermostat from his office is a subtle power play joke. The gag runs through multiple beats (freezing/hot/bayou/sweltering) with escalating absurdity. Solid running gag construction.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You're asking Gilligan not to lie?' / Ira: 'Please call me Ira. I don't like Gilligan.' / Gob: 'Who doesn't like Gilligan?'
jokes.setup: Michael assures Ira that no one is asking him to lie; Gob enters and is immediately suspicious
jokes.punchline: Gob references Gilligan's Island as if no one could dislike the character Gilligan, completely missing that Ira objects to being compared to a bumbling fool
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliantly compact joke. Gob misreads the entire situation, pivots to defending the fictional Gilligan with complete sincerity, and in doing so reveals both his obliviousness and his childlike pop-culture frame of reference. 'Who doesn't like Gilligan?' is perfectly in character for Gob — it's exactly the kind of confident-but-wrong statement he makes. The joke works on Arrested Development's meta-level too (we're literally watching a show about a family's castaway-like dysfunction). High quotability.

jokes.text: Is it cold in here? — George Sr. asks about the temperature via satellite immediately after Ira's name complaint is ignored
jokes.setup: The temperature running gag has been established; Ira's name complaint goes unaddressed
jokes.punchline: George Sr., apparently joining via satellite from prison, pivots back to the temperature complaint, showing he has been listening to the trivial detail while ignoring the financial crisis
jokes.explanation: A competent extension of the running temperature gag. The comedic value comes from George Sr. choosing to weigh in on the most irrelevant possible detail. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I've gotta get rid of her.' / Michael: 'You know, I said two weeks ago that I thought it was a stupid idea. Suddenly Dad says it and it's good advice?'
jokes.setup: Gob says he wants to end his marriage because their father told him it was stupid
jokes.punchline: Michael points out that he said the exact same thing two weeks ago and was ignored — George Sr.'s word is gospel while Michael's identical opinion means nothing
jokes.explanation: Captures the Bluth sibling dynamic efficiently — Gob's slavish devotion to George Sr.'s approval versus his dismissal of Michael is a rich character seam. Well-integrated into theme of the episode (best man/paternal authority). Competent and character-specific.

jokes.text: Gob tells Michael to tell his wife he's 'insensitive and unreliable... something about how you can never confront people. You need someone to clean up your messes.' Michael: 'See, that's great. And that's just off the top of your head.'
jokes.setup: Gob asks Michael to lie to his new wife to break up the marriage; he suggests specific negative character traits to use
jokes.punchline: The traits Gob lists are all accurate descriptions of Michael himself, not lies — and Michael's sarcastic acknowledgment makes it a mutual burn
jokes.explanation: Sharp double-edged joke. Gob inadvertently describes Michael's actual flaws while thinking he's making up slanders. Michael's dry 'just off the top of your head' line perfectly lands the self-aware irony without over-explaining it. Strong character integration — this joke only works with these two specific brothers. The earlier Marta callback ('You had no problem with that') adds additional texture.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I don't want to blame it all on 9/11, but it certainly didn't help.'
jokes.setup: Tobias explains that he and Lindsay have hit a 'rough patch' in their marriage
jokes.punchline: He invokes 9/11 as a contributing factor to their marital troubles — an absurd and self-centered attribution of a national tragedy to his personal problems
jokes.explanation: One of the most quoted lines in the series and one of the sharpest jokes in this episode. The structure is impeccable: the qualifying hedge ('I don't want to blame it ALL on') makes it worse, not better, because it implies 9/11 bears some of the blame. The joke perfectly captures Tobias's narcissistic obliviousness and his pattern of dramatic overstatement. The economy is extraordinary — maximum comedic payload in minimal words. Genuinely exceptional writing. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor and exceeds it on character integration and quotability.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Oh, no.' / Cut to: 'Oh, no was right.' — introducing Dr. Fünke's 100% Natural Good-Time Family-Band Solution
jokes.setup: Tobias mentions wanting to 'reunite the band'; Michael says 'Oh, no'
jokes.punchline: Narrator confirms 'Oh, no was right' and cuts to the band's absurdly over-named title card
jokes.explanation: The narrator editorially agreeing with Michael's 'Oh, no' and then providing the absurd band name is a great use of Arrested Development's omniscient narrator as a comedic device. The narrator breaking from objectivity to offer a value judgment is a recurring stylistic joke that always lands when executed this cleanly.

jokes.text: The full band name: 'Dr. Fünke's 100% Natural Good-Time Family-Band Solution' — and then the corporate ownership chain revealed by the narrator
jokes.setup: Tobias wants to 'reunite the band' — a seemingly quaint family activity
jokes.punchline: The band's name is an absurdly pharmaceutical-sounding title, and the narrator reveals it was underwritten by a labyrinthine corporate chain culminating in 'The Squimm Group' whose motto was simply 'We keep you alive'
jokes.explanation: The joke escalates beautifully: the pharmaceutical band name gives way to the narrator's deadpan corporate genealogy, which gives way to the Squimm Group's ominous/minimal motto 'We keep you alive.' Each element is funnier than the last. The structural escalation is the joke, and it's executed with perfect timing. The rewatch bonus is high because the Squimm Group's motto lands harder when you realize it's a supplement company promising only the bare minimum of wellness.

jokes.text: Zanotab pharmaceutical side effects: 'dry mouth, hair loss, an overly alert feeling, and in some cases may diminish your sex drive'
jokes.setup: The 'wellness' band is revealed to be sponsored by a pharmaceutical supplement company
jokes.punchline: The side effects are read in the rapid deadpan style of real pharmaceutical ads, including the vague 'overly alert feeling' and the relationship-destroying sex drive reduction
jokes.explanation: A sharp parody of pharmaceutical ad disclaimers. The 'overly alert feeling' is a particularly good invention — vague, clinical, and slightly ominous. Pays off when Lindsay later mentions the side effects destroying their marriage. Good structural setup for future payoffs.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I don't know. I probably stole it. I mean, if he says it's missing, it is. Unless he stole it. Hey, maybe he stole it.' / Michael: 'He's the one that said it's missing.' / George Sr.: 'Yeah, I probably stole it.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks his imprisoned father about missing money from company accounts
jokes.punchline: George Sr. casually admits he 'probably stole it,' then deflects to blaming the accountant, then immediately returns to 'yeah, I probably stole it' — the casual admission of guilt treated as a mundane process of elimination
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. The joke works because George Sr. treats financial crimes the way a normal person might wonder if they left the stove on — casual, pragmatic, slightly absentminded. 'I probably stole it' as a default assumption about himself, then the half-hearted attempt to blame Ira, then the resigned return to self-blame — the structure perfectly mirrors his personality. Could only be George Sr. Bluth. The looping back to 'yeah, I probably stole it' makes it land harder on the repeat. Strong candidate for standout moment.

jokes.text: The Ira/IRA/Gilligan confusion escalation: George Sr. promises money will be saved in 'I.R.A.s' / Michael corrects 'It's Ira, sir' / George Sr. says 'Will be saved in Iras' / 'What the hell is this, Gilligan?' / 'It's Ira, sir. Please call me...' / 'Gilligan!'
jokes.setup: The name confusion between Ira (first name) and Gilligan (last name) and IRA (financial account) has been established
jokes.punchline: George Sr. deliberately or obliviously converts Ira's first name back into the financial acronym, and the escalating insistence on calling him 'Gilligan' despite repeated corrections culminates in a shouted 'Gilligan!'
jokes.explanation: This is the payoff of the IRA/Gilligan setup and it's masterfully executed. The joke operates on three simultaneous levels: Gilligan's Island reference, the financial IRA pun, and Ira's increasingly frustrated attempts to assert his identity. George Sr.'s 'will be saved in Iras' is particularly brilliant — he has genuinely (or deliberately) heard Michael's correction and converted it into a new plural financial term. The final shouted 'Gilligan!' after Ira's attempted interjection is perfectly timed cruelty. Among the best structured jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Gilligan has promised me that all this money will be saved in I.R.A.s. / It's Ira, sir. / Oh, I'm sorry, Gilligan. / Will be saved in 'Iras.'
jokes.setup: The Gilligan/Ira name confusion is already established; now George Sr. confuses the acronym IRA (Individual Retirement Account) with Ira as a name
jokes.punchline: George Sr. apologizes for calling him Gilligan, then refers to IRAs as 'Iras' — the name he was just corrected on — creating a perfect loop of confusion
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's tightest piece of wordplay construction. Three separate comedic threads collide: the Gilligan/Ira name confusion, the IRA financial acronym, and George Sr.'s magnificent obliviousness. The joke requires attention to pay off — George Sr. apologizes for 'Gilligan' then immediately says 'Iras,' using the first name he was just corrected on as a plural noun. Exceptional structure. Approaches the upper tier of the scoring scale.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Take him out for some fishing or something. Fun. Guy fun.' / Michael refuses / George Sr.: 'Oh, that's right. You hated fishing.' / Michael: 'No, I didn't.' / George Sr.: 'No, I'll get Gob. He liked it.' / Michael: 'I liked fishing. Where are you going? I'm fun.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. suggests Michael take Ira fishing to get him on their side; Michael refuses on principle
jokes.punchline: George Sr. immediately pivots to saying Michael 'hated fishing' and calling Gob 'the fun one' — Michael's panicked insistence that he does like fishing and IS fun reveals his deep insecurity about being seen as boring
jokes.explanation: A great Michael character beat. His refusal to take Ira fishing is about ethics, but the moment George Sr. calls Gob the 'fun one,' Michael's ethical objection dissolves into wounded pride. Sets up the entire fishing subplot. Strong character integration — this is Michael's core insecurity about being the responsible, unfun one.

jokes.text: Lucille about Annyong's juice box: 'Those are for his soccer team. No sugar for you. You just get more awful.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks about Annyong's juice box at the cocktail party
jokes.punchline: Lucille denies juice to someone (presumably Buster or another family member) with the cutting rationale that sugar makes them 'more awful' — delivered with complete maternal indifference
jokes.explanation: Peak Lucille. The juice is for Annyong's soccer team (not even Annyong's personal supply), and her reason for withholding is a clinical assessment that the recipient gets 'more awful' with sugar. The delivery is maternal cruelty disguised as logic. Very quotable and strongly character-specific.

jokes.text: Buster hitting on Annyong's juice box and the visual of him desperately eyeing a child's juice box
jokes.setup: Lucille has forbidden juice; Buster is at the cocktail party surrounded by adult beverages
jokes.punchline: Buster, a full-grown adult, slinks over to ask his adopted Korean brother if he can 'have a hit' of his juice box — the visual of a grown man desperately coveting a child's juice box is the joke
jokes.explanation: This joke works entirely on visual and character grounds. Buster asking for 'a hit' of Annyong's juice box uses drug-culture language for a child's beverage, which perfectly captures his infantilization. His dependence on sugary drinks (established as a running gag) and his willingness to beg from a child rather than simply drink alcohol like an adult is deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Hey, Adopted Brother. Do you think I could have a hit of that juice box? [reaction beat — Buster clearly steals the juice box from a child]
jokes.setup: Buster, a grown man, is desperate for a juice box that Lucille said belongs to Annyong's soccer team
jokes.punchline: Buster calls Annyong 'Adopted Brother' (highlighting the absurdity of the adoption), then proceeds to beg/steal juice from a small child
jokes.explanation: Buster as a child-man competing with actual children for juice boxes is a strong character beat. 'Hey, Adopted Brother' is a strange, formal way to address a sibling that perfectly captures Buster's social awkwardness. The visual of an adult man coveting a child's juice box is the real joke.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Yeah, well, I was wacked on Zanotab the entire time. I thought Zanotab was supposed to make everything a little bit better.' / Michael: 'For 15 minutes. Then it burns when you pee, and your marriage goes to hell.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to sell Lindsay on rejoining the band; she admits she was medicated the whole time
jokes.punchline: Michael's matter-of-fact clinical description of Zanotab's arc — brief wellness followed by urinary pain and marital collapse — delivered as common knowledge
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff on the pharmaceutical setup. 'For 15 minutes. Then it burns when you pee, and your marriage goes to hell' is a beautifully constructed three-beat joke: the brevity of the good phase, the specific physical consequence, and then the total relationship destruction as a casual afterthought. Michael's delivery of this as established common knowledge makes it even funnier. Very quotable.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'So, uh... You know, if you want me on wood block, I can keep perfect time. Some call me the human metronome.'
jokes.setup: George Michael wants to join the family band and pitches himself
jokes.punchline: He proudly claims his instrument specialty is the wood block and that he's known as 'the human metronome' — an absurdly specific and miniature claim to musical greatness
jokes.explanation: A good George Michael joke — his unearned self-confidence about a trivial skill is perfectly in character. 'Some call me the human metronome' is funny because it implies a reputation he clearly has not earned. The wood block as his instrument of choice is perfectly chosen for maximum comic deflation.

jokes.text: George Michael offers to play wood block and claims to be 'the human metronome' because he's always punctual.
jokes.setup: George Michael wants to join the band and offers his musical skills.
jokes.punchline: 'Some call me the human metronome. You notice how I'm always on time? I'm never late for things.' — he conflates musical rhythm with punctuality as if they're the same skill.
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8+: George Michael's earnest conflation of 'being on time' with 'musical rhythm' is a perfect absurdist character joke. His logic ('it's knowing how long things take') is delivered with complete sincerity and is both wrong and somehow internally coherent. The 'human metronome' self-title is deliciously self-serious for someone whose qualification is showing up on schedule. Very strong character integration — this level of nerdy over-confidence-in-the-wrong-direction is quintessential George Michael.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Yeah, but I think punctuality is slightly different from rhythm.' / George Michael: 'No, it's not. It's the exact same thing. It's knowing how long things take.'
jokes.setup: George Michael claims his punctuality makes him a good percussionist
jokes.punchline: Maeby correctly identifies these as different things; George Michael doubles down with confident but completely wrong logic ('it's knowing how long things take')
jokes.explanation: The callback to the punctuality/rhythm confusion pays off well. George Michael's logical explanation ('it's knowing how long things take') is simultaneously coherent-sounding and completely wrong — it has the internal consistency of a smart person making a dumb argument, which is exactly George Michael's character.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I'm just doing this because if my parents split up, it's a lot more work for me. When they get along, I get tons more alone time.'
jokes.setup: Maeby enthusiastically agrees to join the band
jokes.punchline: Her reason for keeping her parents together is purely selfish and logistical — she needs alone time and a united couple requires less management
jokes.explanation: Maeby's cynical pragmatism about family dynamics is one of her best character traits. The joke works because it inverts the expected sentiment — she's not helping because she loves her family, she's helping because unity is operationally convenient. Strongly character-specific.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'We've been talking here for 28 seconds. Just so you know.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has just delivered his human metronome pitch and been deflated by Maeby
jokes.punchline: He non-sequiturs into announcing the exact elapsed time of their conversation, proving he IS obsessively time-aware — but in the most socially awkward way possible
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The callback is perfectly constructed: George Michael's absurd claim about punctuality being rhythm is proven true in the most literal and socially inappropriate way possible. Announcing that you've been talking for exactly 28 seconds demonstrates the skill while simultaneously proving why it's not a musical skill at all. The economy is exceptional — four words ('just so you know') carry enormous comedic weight. This is exactly the kind of layered callback that makes Arrested Development exceptional. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor and exceeds it.

jokes.text: Fan recognizes Tobias from the band: 'You sang a song about a supplement called Teamocil? I mean, you just nailed me! It was like you knew every side effect I was going through at that time.'
jokes.setup: A convention attendee recognizes Tobias from his wellness band performance in 1996
jokes.punchline: The fan's emotional connection to the band was through relating to the side effects of a pharmaceutical supplement — 'you just nailed me' in the context of pharmaceutical side effects is both the wrong kind of connection and the most genuine one possible
jokes.explanation: Wonderful absurdist payoff. The idea that someone's parasocial connection to a band was forged through shared pharmaceutical side effects rather than musical craft is perfectly executed. 'You just nailed me' has an inadvertent double meaning in the context of pharmaceutical side effects ('diminish your sex drive' from the earlier list). Tobias being celebrated as an artist for accurately depicting pharmaceutical suffering is peak Bluth absurdism.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'The sense of wellness it created in relationships was merely the first sign of complete pituitary shutdown.'
jokes.setup: The fan mourns that Teamocil has been discontinued
jokes.punchline: Tobias explains it was discontinued because the feeling of wellness was actually the first symptom of organ failure
jokes.explanation: A beautifully dark medical joke. The wellness you felt was killing you — the cure was the disease. Tobias delivers this as a medical professional (his background as an analyst-therapist) with complete clinical calm. The word 'merely' does a lot of work, treating complete pituitary shutdown as a minor clarification. Strong structure and dark wit.

jokes.text: Gob's wife: 'You have a terrific voice.' / George Sr. (off-screen or via satellite): 'I smell olives!'
jokes.setup: Michael is meeting Gob's wife and she compliments his voice
jokes.punchline: The romantic/charming moment is punctuated by George Sr. non-sequituring about smelling olives
jokes.explanation: A good absurdist non-sequitur that interrupts a would-be romantic beat. The olive detail is specific enough to be funny without being explained. George Sr.'s complete irrelevance to the social moment is in character. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Gob's wife tells Michael 'Are you hitting on me? Gob said that would happen.' — after Michael was trying to convince her Gob is unreliable.
jokes.setup: Michael is awkwardly trying to talk Gob's wife out of the marriage per Gob's request.
jokes.punchline: She interprets his awkward attentiveness as flirting, which is (a) exactly what Gob predicted and (b) the opposite of what Michael intended.
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — the situation is ironic (Gob sent Michael to sabotage the relationship; instead Michael appears to be making a pass). 'Gob said that would happen' adds a layer of Gob having correctly predicted his own brother's awkwardness, which is a character joke at Michael's expense.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Hey, Gob. It's a good thing you're not staying married to this girl. You've got her hating me... at least your version of me, which is actually you.'
jokes.setup: Gob asked Michael to tell his wife negative things about him (Gob); Michael apparently made himself the subject of the slanders instead
jokes.punchline: The version of Gob that Michael described is actually Michael — because the negative traits Gob listed ('insensitive, unreliable, can't confront people') are Michael's own traits, and Michael apparently described himself
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback payoff. Michael's attempt to follow Gob's instructions resulted in him accurately self-describing, confirming that Gob's 'made-up' slanders were real Michael traits. The 'your version of me, which is actually you' phrasing is a lovely piece of confused identity comedy.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I've got the marriage and none of the good parts. It's like, so far it's been all chain and no ball.'
jokes.setup: Gob is reconsidering his marriage after initially wanting out
jokes.punchline: He inverts the 'ball and chain' metaphor for marriage — instead of being burdened, he has only the burdens and none of the (sexual) benefits, delivered as if this is a reasonable complaint
jokes.explanation: A sharp inversion of a cliché that reveals Gob's priorities with economy and wit. 'All chain and no ball' is a genuinely clever reformulation that works on the literal/metaphorical level simultaneously. Strong quotability. Perfectly Gob — his complaint about marriage is entirely about what he's not getting physically.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I guess being your brother and solving all your problems for you doesn't qualify me as your best man.'
jokes.setup: Gob announces he's making their father his best man
jokes.punchline: Michael's wounded sarcasm — listing genuine qualifications (brotherhood, problem-solving) while the joke is that these are EXACTLY the qualifications for best man and yet he's still passed over
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment but not exceptional. Michael's wounded pride is well-played but the joke is fairly predictable given the setup. Works as character drama more than pure comedy.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Don't worry. You're still...' / Michael: 'What? Invited?' / Gob: 'No, I was gonna say you're still the guy I want solving my problems, but, yeah... Let me talk to Dad about that invite situation.'
jokes.setup: Gob starts to reassure Michael with 'Don't worry, you're still—'
jokes.punchline: Michael assumes he means 'invited' (a low bar); Gob reveals he was actually going to say something nicer, but then casually implies Michael might not be invited at all, as an afterthought
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly structured three-beat joke. Beat 1: Gob starts something reassuring. Beat 2: Michael assumes the bare minimum (being invited). Beat 3: Gob reveals he was going to say something better — but then casually throws in 'Let me talk to Dad about that invite situation,' implying the bar Michael set (just being invited) might also be at risk. The structure is exceptional — two misdirections in one joke. Strong Gob character work.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'No, no. Your bachelor party's about convincing Gilligan that he's killed a stripper, getting him out of town so he can't testify, and making sure that people think that he took the money.'
jokes.setup: Gob thinks his bachelor party is about having fun
jokes.punchline: George Sr. reveals the bachelor party is actually an elaborate criminal scheme involving a fake stripper death, witness intimidation, and evidence planting — delivered with the same casual authority as a party planning suggestion
jokes.explanation: The reveal that the bachelor party is a criminal conspiracy is perfectly executed. George Sr. lists witness tampering, evidence planting, and inducing a fake murder confession with the same tone as 'we'll have a bounce house.' The specificity ('corn syrup, mixed with a little red dye') makes it funnier. Strong George Sr. character work — his criminal enterprise operates on the same cheerful register as normal family events.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'That's why I want you to use this stripper — a narcoleptic, believe it or not. A friend of mine at the yacht club, he used her for his bachelor party. We wanted you to have the first piece!'
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains the mechanics of the scheme
jokes.punchline: The stripper is narcoleptic — which is simultaneously the specific practical tool needed AND an absurd detail about her as a person. 'We wanted you to have the first piece' implies the yacht club circulates a narcoleptic stripper as a privilege among members
jokes.explanation: 'We wanted you to have the first piece' is the punchline — the narcoleptic stripper is a yacht club amenity, shared among members like a coveted vintage or club resource. The sentence implies a waiting list, a booking system, a community of men who've used this same person. The absurdity is in the logistics implied by that single phrase. Good dark comedy with strong originality.

jokes.text: That's why I want you to use this stripper... a narcoleptic, believe it or not. A friend of mine at the yacht club, he used her for his bachelor party. / We wanted you to have the first piece!
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains the narcoleptic stripper scheme
jokes.punchline: 'We wanted you to have the first piece!' — delivered as a gift, as if sharing a favorite restaurant recommendation, about a narcoleptic woman used as a prop
jokes.explanation: The cheerful enthusiasm of 'We wanted you to have the first piece!' applied to a narcoleptic stripper is darkly funny. George Sr.'s affectionate crime-mentoring relationship with his sons is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: George Sr. demonstrates the fake death plan with sound effects: 'He comes to. Eee! Blood! Aaah! He freaks out.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is explaining the elaborate fake blood scheme to convince Ira he killed a stripper
jokes.punchline: The normally composed criminal patriarch performs the accountant's panicked reaction with sound effects and gestures — the contrast between his usual gravitas and his theatrical pantomime of a man freaking out
jokes.explanation: The humor comes from George Sr. performing this theatrical pantomime while presumably still in prison, delivering the sound effects ('Eee! Blood! Aaah!') with complete confidence in his plan. His comfort with criminal scheming shown through the planning detail is well-executed character comedy.

jokes.text: Lindsay accidentally takes Euphorazine instead of Zanotab; Tobias: 'We are pushers, not takers.'
jokes.setup: The family band rehearsal is interrupted because Lindsay has taken the wrong supplement
jokes.punchline: 'We are pushers, not takers' — Tobias's rationalization that their pharmaceutical promotion is noble because they distribute rather than consume, delivered as a moral position
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'We are pushers, not takers' uses drug-dealer terminology ('pushers') as if it's the morally superior position — they're dealers, not users, which is presented as a distinction worth making. The economy is perfect: five words that simultaneously reference pharmaceutical wellness culture, drug culture, and Tobias's complete lack of self-awareness. High quotability. Among the best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Let's take it from loose stool! One, two and a...' then stops to confront Lindsay about Euphorazine
jokes.setup: Tobias is directing the family band rehearsal
jokes.punchline: 'Let's take it from loose stool' — apparently one of the band's song/section names, which sounds like a pharmaceutical side effect (and probably is one), shouted as an enthusiastic musical direction
jokes.explanation: A great callback and visual gag — 'loose stool' is both a genuine pharmaceutical side effect and apparently a named section of Dr. Fünke's 100% Natural Good-Time Family-Band Solution's set. Tobias shouting it with the enthusiasm of 'let's take it from the top' is the joke. The rewatch bonus is strong because it confirms the band's entire repertoire is pharmaceutical side effects.

jokes.text: What is that noise? / We don't need the wood block. / No, I just thought I would join in on the fun.
jokes.setup: George Michael has inserted himself into band rehearsal with the wood block despite being excluded
jokes.punchline: His joining is so disruptive that Tobias — who desperately wants family togetherness — rejects even this attempt at inclusion
jokes.explanation: The irony of Tobias, who is literally trying to create family bonding, rejecting George Michael's attempt to join is good cringe. But the beat is quick and doesn't fully develop.

jokes.text: Michael's pitch for the fishing trip: 'We get to stay at a hotel, get up at 4:00 a.m., slap on the old seasick patch. We're gonna get out there on the choppy ocean. We're gonna catch ourselves a little lunch.' / George Michael: 'Oh, we get to eat it?' / Michael: 'After we gut it.'
jokes.setup: Michael enthusiastically pitches the fishing trip as fun, listing details that are increasingly unpleasant
jokes.punchline: George Michael's hopeful 'we get to eat it?' followed by Michael's cheerful 'after we gut it' — the pitch keeps revealing new unpleasantnesses while Michael's delivery insists they're selling points
jokes.explanation: The escalating sales pitch for an increasingly miserable experience is well-executed. Michael's insistence that each unpleasant element is a feature ('get up at 4:00 a.m.!') while George Michael's enthusiasm steadily dims is good father-son character work. 'After we gut it' as the final reveal in a supposedly fun list lands cleanly.

jokes.text: We get to stay at a hotel, get up at 4:00 a.m., slap on the old seasick patch. / We're gonna get out there on the choppy ocean. We're gonna catch ourselves a little lunch. / Oh, we get to eat it? / After we gut it.
jokes.setup: Michael is selling the fishing trip as fun; each detail is progressively more unpleasant
jokes.punchline: George Michael's hope ('Oh, we get to eat it?') is immediately dashed ('After we gut it') — the 'fun' trip sounds like moderate punishment
jokes.explanation: The escalating unpleasantness of Michael's 'fun' pitch (4 AM, seasick patches, choppy water) is perfectly observed, and George Michael's one moment of hope ('Oh, we get to eat it?') being immediately negated by 'After we gut it' is a satisfying little beat.

jokes.text: Lucille spelling out J-U-l-C-E / Buster: 'I can spell, Mom. You spelled juice.' / Lucille: 'Oh, you're so brilliant. Let's see you find it.'
jokes.setup: Lucille spells out J-U-I-C-E to prevent Buster from understanding she's hiding juice from him
jokes.punchline: Buster correctly decodes it immediately; Lucille's response isn't embarrassment but a new challenge — she acknowledges his intelligence sarcastically and then points out he still can't find the juice
jokes.explanation: Lucille spelling juice around her adult son like he's a toddler is inherently funny. Buster decoding it correctly provides a rare competence moment — but Lucille's pivot to 'let's see you find it' is the real joke, showing her control isn't through concealment but through the actual physical hiding of juice. The sarcastic 'oh, you're so brilliant' is peak Lucille-Buster dynamic.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Besides, I'm going to Annyong's soccer award ceremony... And I don't need the other soccer moms knowing how old my first batch of kids are.'
jokes.setup: Lucille explains why Buster must go to Gob's bachelor party instead of staying home
jokes.punchline: 'My first batch of kids' — reducing her adult biological children to a manufacturing metaphor, implying Annyong is a newer, better batch
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Lucille line. 'My first batch of kids' is a devastatingly precise piece of character writing — it reduces her adult children to a production run, implies they're a product she'd rather not have associated with her current brand, and reveals her vanity (she doesn't want soccer moms to know how old she is) all in one phrase. Highly quotable. Could only be Lucille Bluth.

jokes.text: Lucille: '— Annyong's...' / Annyong: 'Annyong.' / Lucille: 'Would somebody please tell this insufferable child to... God!'
jokes.setup: Lucille keeps triggering Annyong to say his name by saying his name
jokes.punchline: Lucille reaches her breaking point at 'Annyong' responding to being named, unable to complete a sentence about him without him responding — her frustrated 'God!' is the payoff
jokes.explanation: A solid iteration of the Annyong running gag. The joke is structural — Lucille literally cannot talk about him without him responding. Her exasperation is in character and well-played. The joke works better for viewers who've been watching the series and know the full Annyong gag.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Not everything is strippers and booze and buckets of blood.' / George Michael: 'Why do you guys have buckets of blood?' / Gob: 'It's not real blood. It's corn syrup and red dye.' / Buster: 'Juice.' / Gob: 'We have unlimited juice? This party is gonna be off the hook.'
jokes.setup: The hotel room encounter where Michael discovers the bachelor party paraphernalia
jokes.punchline: Three rapid-fire beats: George Michael's innocent confusion about the blood, Gob's clinical explanation, and then Buster mishearing 'red dye' as 'juice' and becoming ecstatically excited about 'unlimited juice'
jokes.explanation: One of the best joke sequences in the episode — possibly the series. Three characters, three perfect character beats in rapid succession. George Michael's genuine confusion ('why do you guys have buckets of blood?') is the straight-man setup. Gob's clinical matter-of-fact correction about the blood composition is the secondary laugh. But Buster's mishearing — hearing 'red dye' as juice, then learning it's UNLIMITED juice, and declaring the party 'off the hook' — is a perfect convergence of character, callback, and structure. Every element serves every other element. The corn syrup plan introduced earlier pays off in Buster's juice obsession from earlier. Exceptional craft.

jokes.text: Maeby reads the new Euphorazine side effects list and reacts to 'Delayed irritability': 'What do they mean by delayed?' — then immediately snaps: 'It means it comes later! This is ridiculous! Why do we even have to do this?'
jokes.setup: Maeby reads 'delayed irritability' as a side effect of the supplement she's apparently already taken
jokes.punchline: She asks what 'delayed' means, then immediately demonstrates it — her irritability isn't delayed, it arrives as she processes the word 'delayed'
jokes.explanation: This is a structurally perfect joke. The setup and punchline are the same moment — Maeby's question 'what do they mean by delayed?' is immediately answered by her own delayed-becoming-immediate irritability. The joke is self-demonstrating. It requires zero explanation because it IS its own explanation. The structure is as close to perfect as jokes get — the premise and the punchline are simultaneous. Among the best-constructed jokes in the episode. The rewatch bonus is high because you appreciate the precision of the construction more on second viewing.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I'm tired of trying to find happiness through lies and self-medicating.' / [immediately goes to the bar]
jokes.setup: Lindsay delivers a sincere speech about being done with pharmaceutical happiness and fakery
jokes.punchline: She immediately goes to the bar — trading one form of self-medication for another, contradicting her stated position within seconds
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom irony but executed with Lindsay-specific character integration. The joke is in the immediate action following the sincerity — she doesn't even finish her speech before going to the bar. The contrast between the earnest self-awareness and the instant self-contradiction is Lindsay in a nutshell.

jokes.text: Wellness convention organizer to Tobias: 'You think you can handle it alone? 'Cause if you don't perform, we don't validate.'
jokes.setup: The convention organizer pressures Tobias to perform despite his family abandoning him
jokes.punchline: 'We don't validate' — in context means parking validation, but to Tobias (a therapist/analyst) 'validation' has a profound psychological meaning; he's also a person who desperately needs emotional validation
jokes.explanation: A well-executed double meaning. The parking validation reading is literal and mercenary; the psychological validation reading is tragic and perfect for Tobias. The line lands on both levels simultaneously, and the fact that parking is likely what the organizer means makes it funnier — Tobias is being threatened with a parking fee as a substitute for emotional acceptance. Good economy and strong character integration.

jokes.text: Michael to George Michael: 'They think they're having fun, Son, but it's not lasting fun. It is fleeting fun, like a bachelor party. What we got here is lasting fun.'
jokes.setup: They can hear the pool party happening while lying in the dark hotel room at 10 PM to prepare for a 4 AM wake-up call
jokes.punchline: Michael's earnest philosophical distinction between 'fleeting fun' (which sounds fun) and 'lasting fun' (which they're currently not having) is visually undercut by the obvious misery of their situation
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the obvious gap between Michael's philosophical framework and their actual experience. 'Lasting fun' is code for 'not fun at the time.' His sincere belief that lying in the dark is superior to a pool party is perfect Michael — he intellectualizes his own joylessness into a virtue.

jokes.text: Good night, Son. / Good night, Dad. / Marco! / Polo! / Marco! / Polo! / Marco!
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael settle in for a good night's sleep at 10pm to be ready for 4 AM
jokes.punchline: The Marco Polo game outside immediately mocks their 'lasting fun' pretension — the pool party's childlike game contrasts with their forced grown-up responsible fun
jokes.explanation: The Marco Polo game is a perfectly chosen sound — it's the sound of people having actual, uncomplicated fun that Michael told his son is inferior. The acoustic irony works well.

jokes.text: Marco Polo game being played at the bachelor party pool, overheard by Michael and George Michael in their hotel room at bedtime
jokes.setup: Michael has just proclaimed the fishing trip to be 'lasting fun' versus the 'fleeting fun' of the party
jokes.punchline: The Marco Polo calls from the pool punctuate Michael's speech — the specific choice of Marco Polo (a children's game being played by adults at a bachelor party) emphasizes how much fun everyone else is having
jokes.explanation: The Marco Polo game is a functional contrast joke — the specific game chosen is good (childishly fun, loud, festive) but the joke isn't exceptional. It reinforces the scene's comedy but doesn't stand alone.

jokes.text: George Michael's elaborate excuse to go check on Maeby's band: 'I have a pretty finely tuned internal clock... which is why I'm actually such a good natural percussionist... I'm just worried I'm not gonna be able to fall asleep for another two hours and 45 minutes.'
jokes.setup: George Michael wants to go to Maeby's band performance instead of sleeping
jokes.punchline: He invokes his 'internal clock' (the human metronome bit) as the reason he can't sleep — claiming the same neurotic time-awareness that he presented as a skill is now a burden
jokes.explanation: A good callback to the metronome bit. George Michael's internal clock is now both his claimed musical gift and his insomnia diagnosis — the same trait simultaneously justifies his band membership pitch and his inability to sleep. The specificity of '45 minutes' in 'two hours and 45 minutes' is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Michael's manipulation: 'I'd hate to miss it, because I was too proud.' / George Michael: 'Wait a minute. Too proud? What does this have to do with too proud?' / Michael: 'Actually, that part was kinda just for you. I was just worried the whole thing wasn't gonna land unless I included the pride part.'
jokes.setup: Michael is giving a speech to manipulate George Michael into going to the bachelor party
jokes.punchline: George Michael catches the manipulation, and Michael openly admits he added the 'pride' element purely for rhetorical effect — transparently revealing his speech-craft in real time
jokes.explanation: Exceptional meta-comedy. Michael essentially deconstructs his own manipulation in real time, admitting that the 'pride' element was a rhetorical device inserted because he wasn't sure 'the whole thing was gonna land.' This is meta-theatrical self-awareness applied to a parenting moment — Michael is workshopping his speech while delivering it. The honesty of the admission (and the fact that it then presumably DOES land) is layers of brilliance. Among the very best jokes in the episode. High quotability and rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Actually, that part was kinda just for you. I was just worried the whole thing wasn't gonna land unless I included the pride part.
jokes.setup: Michael catches onto George Michael's manipulation — 'I'd hate to miss it because I was too proud'
jokes.punchline: George Michael openly admits he added the 'pride' element as a rhetorical device to make his excuse more effective — meta-commentary on joke construction from a teenager
jokes.explanation: A remarkably sophisticated meta-joke where George Michael deconstructs his own persuasion attempt in real time — 'I was just worried the whole thing wasn't gonna land.' This is the show commenting on its own joke structure through a teenager who speaks in sitcom logic. The character integration is exceptional: George Michael is the one character smart enough to do this, and the moment reveals his calculating side beneath the awkward exterior. Among the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: 'Who called the cops?' / Narrator: 'And with the aid of some helpful gays.'
jokes.setup: Gob's bachelor party is being set up; the narrator mentions help from 'helpful gays'
jokes.punchline: Gob says 'Who called the cops?' — the Hot Cops (male stripper cops) have arrived, but his confused 'who called the cops' reads as if he thinks they're real police. The narrator's 'helpful gays' is a play on 'helpful gaze' or possibly a deliberate correction of a homophone
jokes.explanation: A classic AD narrator joke — the narrator corrects or reframes what a character says in a way that changes the meaning. 'Helpful gaze' becomes 'helpful gays' as the narrator specifies. The Hot Cops concept (police uniform strippers) is also a running AD gag. Good economy and the rewatch bonus rewards attention.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You told me to hire people to look like my friends. They're Hot Cops. They're entertainers. Although I specifically asked them not to come dressed as cops.' / George Sr.: 'You better change now... Butch, guys. I want it butch.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. expected regular-looking party guests; Gob hired Hot Cops
jokes.punchline: Gob's logic ('you said to hire people who look like my friends' → therefore male strippers in police costumes) followed by George Sr.'s direction of 'I want it butch' — his solution to male strippers in cop uniforms is to make them more masculine
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy. Gob's logic chain (hire people who look like your friends = Hot Cops) is absurd but internally consistent with his character. George Sr.'s tactical response — not 'send them away' but 'make them more butch' — is both funny and says something about his priorities.

jokes.text: Buster has found and apparently drunk the fake blood (corn syrup and red dye) — 'Hey, Brother. Somebody put on his lipstick in the car.' / Buster: 'I love juice!'
jokes.setup: Gob prepared two buckets of corn-syrup-and-red-dye fake blood for the scheme; they're missing
jokes.punchline: Buster has drunk both buckets of fake blood, mistaking it for juice, and has red all around his mouth — the visual of an adult man with a blood-red mouth happily declaring 'I love juice!' while his brother notes he 'put on his lipstick'
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and one of the finest in the series. This is the convergence of THREE separate running gags: (1) Buster's juice obsession, (2) the corn syrup fake blood plan, (3) Buster's belief there would be 'unlimited juice' at the party. All three threads were planted carefully across 12 minutes of the episode and converge here perfectly. The visual of Buster with red all over his mouth, described as 'lipstick' by Gob (implying feminization of his childlike behavior), while declaring 'I love juice!' is maximally funny. This is Arrested Development at its structural best — a deeply earned punchline that requires every prior setup to land. Comparable to the 9.5/10 anchor examples.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I must say I feel a bit like a Mary... without a Peter and a Paul, but I suppose it's worth a shot.'
jokes.setup: Tobias prepares to perform solo despite his family leaving
jokes.punchline: 'A Mary without a Peter and a Paul' — a reference to Peter, Paul and Mary (the folk trio) but also: Mary is typically the 'feminine' member, 'Peter' and 'Paul' are male names, and Tobias identifying as the Mary implies his gender identification pattern (AD's long-running Tobias-is-secretly-gay joke)
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias wordplay that operates on multiple levels. Surface level: he's missing his trio partners. Musical reference level: Peter, Paul and Mary. Character level: Tobias identifies as the feminine member (Mary) and laments the absence of Peters (male) — a continuation of AD's long-running joke about Tobias's repressed homosexuality. The 'but I suppose it's worth a shot' undercuts the pathos with his characteristic oblivious optimism. Among the best Tobias lines of the episode.

jokes.text: 'Teamocil is no longer available. Please try either Grouphug, Bondat or consult your own Wellness Guide.' / Teamocil!
jokes.setup: Tobias has performed the Teamocil song solo; a fan sees the supplement disclaimer on screen
jokes.punchline: The fan yells 'Teamocil!' as if at a rock concert — treating a pharmaceutical safety warning like an encore demand
jokes.explanation: The pharmaceutical-as-rock-concert moment is a tight satirical beat. The fan screaming 'Teamocil!' like a band name at a concert perfectly captures both wellness culture fanaticism and the show's ongoing pharmaceutical satire.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'You're out of the band.' — after the Teamocil sign flashes during Tobias's performance
jokes.setup: Tobias has just performed his pharmaceutical supplement ballad to an audience that came to see a wellness product discontinued
jokes.punchline: The narrator editorializes again, declaring Tobias 'out of the band' as if there's still a band to be out of — the absurdity of the narrator taking a position on something that's already collapsed
jokes.explanation: The narrator's editorial intrusion is a reliable AD device. 'You're out of the band' is funny because (a) the band barely existed, (b) Tobias has already been abandoned, (c) the narrator is imposing further punishment on an already defeated man. Good economy.

jokes.text: Ira: 'It's Ira, and I'm fine.' — in response to being greeted at the party, still objecting to the Gilligan name while trying to seem unbothered
jokes.setup: Ira arrives at the bachelor party and is presumably greeted as Gilligan
jokes.punchline: His response packages both the name correction AND a social pleasantry into one phrase — 'It's Ira, and I'm fine' — showing his resignation and the compulsive correction has become automatic
jokes.explanation: A good iteration of the running name gag. The combination of name correction and social pleasantry in one breath suggests Ira has had to do this so many times it's become a reflex. Good economy.

jokes.text: Buster discovered the narcoleptic stripper inside a birthday cake in the next room and apparently triggered her narcolepsy — visual of Buster with what looks like blood around his mouth after having drunk the fake blood earlier
jokes.setup: The narcoleptic stripper is hidden in a cake; Buster is wandering through the hotel looking for sugar
jokes.punchline: Buster, already covered in red (from the 'juice'), discovers the stripper — the narrator tells us he was 'unaware of the narcoleptic stripper confined within it,' implying he found the cake and began eating it while the woman was inside
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed visual comedy chain. Buster's sugar-seeking behavior has led him to discover the stripper's cake, and his red-stained appearance (from the 'juice') sets up the later moment where the stripper thinks she's caused blood. Good structural payoff that bridges multiple earlier setups.

jokes.text: Michael arrives at the bachelor party: 'I'm here to support him, like all these, uh, hot men and Ira.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the bachelor party to find it populated by Hot Cops and Ira
jokes.punchline: He registers the Hot Cops with visible confusion, tries to find inclusive language, and lands on 'hot men' — acknowledging their attractiveness in the most uncomfortable way possible — before tacking on 'and Ira' as an afterthought
jokes.explanation: Michael's discomfort with the Hot Cops expressed through awkward inclusive language is well-played. 'Hot men' is funnier than 'male strippers' because it shows Michael trying to be respectful/neutral and landing on something even more loaded. The 'and Ira' addition perfectly separates the accountant from the entertainment.

jokes.text: Well, you're not gonna stay. I mean, this isn't your kind of scene. / Dad, please, stay out of it, all right? I'm here for Gob. Now, where are the strippers?
jokes.setup: George Sr. tries to exclude Michael from the party; Michael asserts himself
jokes.punchline: Michael, in asserting his right to be here, asks 'where are the strippers?' — the very thing George Sr. assumed he'd object to
jokes.explanation: Michael overcorrecting in his assertion of fun — demanding strippers to prove he's not a killjoy — is a satisfying character beat. The reversal works well.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Well, you're not gonna stay. I mean, this isn't your kind of scene.' / Michael: 'Dad, please, stay out of it, all right? I'm here for Gob. Now, where are the strippers?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. tells Michael this isn't his scene
jokes.punchline: Michael's defensive response immediately asks for the strippers — proving he's overcorrecting his 'no fun' reputation by diving into exactly the bachelor party atmosphere he'd normally avoid
jokes.explanation: The joke lands because Michael's 'I'm fun' insecurity reaches its logical extreme — he's now demanding strippers to prove a point. 'Now, where are the strippers?' from Michael Bluth is inherently funny because it's so out of character for his established persona.

jokes.text: Michael discovers the back room and sees what appears to be a dead body / stripper: 'Huh? Huh? Huh? I can handle a back room.' — then sees the stripper and immediately says 'I'm calling the cops.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just declared he can 'handle a back room' while proving he's fun
jokes.punchline: The instant he sees the (apparently dead) stripper his instinct is to call the cops — abandoning his 'fun' performance immediately when confronted with actual stakes
jokes.explanation: The speed of Michael's reversal from 'I can handle a back room' to 'I'm calling the cops' is the joke. It confirms his essential nature — the moment actual consequences appear, the fun persona evaporates instantly. Well-paced and character-specific.

jokes.text: Gob: 'She died. She's dead.' / George Sr.: 'It was Gilligan!' / Ira: 'What?' / Gob: 'Gilligan killed the skipper... stripper.'
jokes.setup: The scheme is being executed — Gob and George Sr. are trying to convince Ira he killed the stripper
jokes.punchline: Gob's Freudian slip: 'Gilligan killed the skipper... stripper' — accidentally completing the Gilligan's Island reference (Skipper was Gilligan's companion on the show) before correcting himself
jokes.explanation: Arguably the single best joke in the episode. 'Gilligan killed the skipper... stripper' is a perfect triple-layered joke: (1) the Freudian slip reveals the writers' entire naming scheme was building to this moment, (2) it completes the Gilligan's Island reference (Skipper! His partner on the show!), (3) Gob catching himself and correcting to 'stripper' is funnier than if he'd said it right. This has been building since the opening seconds of the episode. It rewards attentive viewers, it's quotable, it's deeply layered, and the 'skipper... stripper' correction lands like a perfectly thrown punchline. This is Arrested Development's structural genius in one line. Exceeds the 8.0 anchor significantly. Among the best jokes in the series.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Ira, take my honeymoon tickets. Get out of the country. Save yourself.' / Ira: 'I'm not drunk. Bix made me the designated driver.'
jokes.setup: The scheme requires Ira to be drunk; Gob tries to convince him he's incapacitated
jokes.punchline: Ira's simple factual rebuttal — 'I'm not drunk. Bix made me the designated driver' — collapses the entire scheme with one sentence. The name 'Bix' as his source of authority is an additional absurd detail
jokes.explanation: The entire criminal scheme collapses because Ira is the designated driver — a completely mundane, responsible choice that George Sr.'s elaborate plan failed to account for. The specificity of 'Bix made me' (Bix being a named Hot Cop) suggests Ira has been responsibly avoiding alcohol while the Bluths assumed he was getting drunk. Good comic deflation.

jokes.text: The narcoleptic stripper wakes up, sees Buster with red around his mouth, and thinks she killed him for groping her. Narrator: 'Not again.'
jokes.setup: The stripper was supposed to fake death; instead she wakes up and sees what appears to be a man she killed
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'Not again' implies this has happened before — the narcoleptic stripper has woken up next to apparent murder victims multiple times. It also confirms Buster groped her, which triggered her defensive response
jokes.explanation: Two words that do extraordinary work. 'Not again' implies an entire backstory for this stripper — she has a pattern of waking up next to apparent murder victims, possibly related to her narcolepsy causing her to be unconscious during altercations. It's also darkly funny about Buster — someone groped her and she apparently responded with force, and Buster's red mouth (from the 'juice') looks like blood. The economy is exceptional. A masterclass in narrator comedy.

jokes.text: Well, the hell with you, sir. Bix, we're leaving. / I'm testifying.
jokes.setup: Ira realizes he's been set up; he delivers his verdict
jokes.punchline: 'I'm testifying' — delivered as the ultimate consequence, the most damning two words an accountant can say to a criminal family, stated with complete calm
jokes.explanation: The understated delivery of 'I'm testifying' after a chaotic scheme has failed is a good deadpan button. 'Well, the hell with you, sir' as formal outrage is a nice character note.

jokes.text: Michael gestures for Gob to punch George Sr. / George Sr.: 'Oh, no. No, no. Don't you dare, mister.' / Gob punches him.
jokes.setup: Michael encourages Gob to stand up to George Sr. after it's revealed the whole scheme was George Sr.'s idea
jokes.punchline: Michael's wordless encouragement leads to Gob actually punching his father — a direct physical consequence of Michael telling Gob to 'start trusting himself'
jokes.explanation: The punch is well-earned — Gob following through on the gesture is character-consistent (he's impulsive) and the 'Don't you dare, mister' from George Sr. (using parental language to his adult son) adds a good beat before the impact. Solid physical comedy.

jokes.text: Even if it was my idea, which it wasn't, Gob screwed it up, which he did and always does.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is caught out as the architect of the scheme
jokes.punchline: He simultaneously denies responsibility AND fully assigns blame — the parenthetical '(which he did and always does)' about Gob screwing up is delivered with detached certainty
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed sentence of denial and condemnation. The parenthetical structure — 'which it wasn't... which he did and always does' — mimics legal hedging while being damning in both directions. Pure George Sr.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I might be a father.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob 'You might not be a father, but you are my brother'
jokes.punchline: Gob quietly responds 'I might be a father' — a deeply casual revelation of a possible child that he's apparently been sitting on
jokes.explanation: Four words of perfect economy. Michael's brotherly speech includes 'you might not be a father' as a rhetorical device to compliment Gob — and Gob's quiet, sincere 'I might be a father' takes the rhetoric literally. It's simultaneously an accidental revelation, a character moment (Gob doesn't understand metaphors), and a piece of foreshadowing. The juxtaposition of Michael's earnest speechifying against Gob's oblivious literal interpretation is perfectly timed. Maximum economy — possibly the most words-per-laugh efficient joke in the episode.

jokes.text: Gob: 'You're the best man. You're my best man.' / Michael: 'Not if I have an actual ceremony, 'cause Dad would kill me, but...'
jokes.setup: In a genuine brotherly moment, Gob declares Michael his best man
jokes.punchline: Michael's immediate qualification — 'not if I have an actual ceremony, 'cause Dad would kill me' — punctures the sentiment by reverting immediately to family politics and George Sr.'s authority
jokes.explanation: A good deflation of the brotherly moment — the Bluths can't sustain sentiment for more than a sentence. Michael's caveat shows his ingrained deference to George Sr. even in his most independent moments. Functional character comedy.

jokes.text: You're the best man. You're my best man. / Not if I have an actual ceremony, 'cause Dad would kill me, but...
jokes.setup: Michael delivers a sincere brotherly speech; Gob responds warmly
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately adds the caveat that this only applies informally — at a real ceremony, Dad's authority would override it
jokes.explanation: The sweet moment is immediately undercut by Gob's practical cowardice about George Sr. — a good character button that keeps the sentiment from getting too sincere.

jokes.text: Gob's wife bursts in: 'Gob, I want out. I'm in love with your brother.' — then corrects: 'In-law. Tobias. Sorry. I should have finished that thought.'
jokes.setup: The emotional resolution is interrupted by Gob's wife wanting to end the marriage.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm in love with your brother' — pause — 'In-law. Tobias.' The unsaid implication (Michael) is allowed to hang before the correction, and then the correction (Tobias) is its own punchline.
jokes.explanation: Justifies 8.5+: Perfect misdirection structure — 'I'm in love with your brother' (implying Michael) → correction: 'In-law. Tobias.' The correction is funnier than the original implication because Tobias is such an unlikely romantic choice. 'Sorry. I should have finished that thought' as a grace note is impeccably timed. Gob's 'You did it again, you son of a bitch!' to Michael is an extra layer — he blames Michael even though Michael did nothing. Highly quotable and rewatch-rewarding.

jokes.text: Gob's wife: 'Gob, I want out. I'm in love with your brother.' / Gob to Michael: 'You did it again, you son of a bitch!' / Michael: 'No, no! She's...' / Gob: 'In-law. Tobias. Sorry. I should have finished that thought.'
jokes.setup: Gob's wife dramatically declares she's 'in love with your brother' — implying Michael
jokes.punchline: The reveal: she means Tobias (Gob's brother-in-law, not Michael). 'In-law. Tobias. Sorry. I should have finished that thought.' — the incomplete sentence caused the misdirection, and the correction is delivered with completely inappropriate casualness
jokes.explanation: A perfect misdirection joke with an exceptional structural callback. 'I'm in love with your brother' lands as Michael, which makes Gob's reaction ('you did it again, you son of a bitch!') perfectly in character — referencing Michael's history of romantic involvement with Gob's loves (Marta). But the actual reveal — she means Tobias, her brother-in-law — is both funnier and more absurd. 'I should have finished that thought' as an apology for causing a near-family-catastrophe is delivered with devastating understatement. Among the very best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: 'On the next Arrested Development...' / Lucille: 'Oh my god. I killed Michael.' / Gob: 'Yeah, but on the plus side, you got punched in the face.'
jokes.setup: The 'On the next...' preview sequence begins with a shocking revelation
jokes.punchline: Gob's consolation for George Sr. (who was punched) framed as a positive — 'on the plus side, you got punched in the face' — inverting the normal comfort structure
jokes.explanation: The 'on the plus side, you got punched in the face' structure is a great inversion of comfort-giving. Normally 'on the plus side' precedes something good; here the 'plus' IS the punch. Gob framing the assault he committed as a gift to his father is excellent character comedy. Also, Lucille's 'I killed Michael' as an opening tease is effectively dark — we don't know what happened, which makes the parenthetical Gob joke funnier by contrast.

jokes.text: 'And Gilligan... fulfills his destiny.' — cut to Ira on a desert island (implied), phone ringing, narrator: 'Let it ring.'
jokes.setup: The preview sequence shows Michael solving the mystery of the missing money by connecting Ira to all the accounts
jokes.punchline: 'Gilligan fulfills his destiny' — implying he ends up stranded/isolated like the Gilligan's Island character. 'Let it ring' suggests he's now ignoring calls, either out of fear or because he's followed through on disappearing. The entire Gilligan's Island metaphor completes itself in the tag
jokes.explanation: A perfect structural coda. The entire episode's naming joke pays off in the final tag — 'Gilligan fulfills his destiny' is the narrator completing the metaphor that's been running since the opening seconds. Ira Gilligan, named after the hapless castaway, ends up stranded/isolated just like his namesake. 'Let it ring' as the final line implies he's now out of reach, like a castaway. The structure is exceptional — this is a joke that was 21 minutes in the making. High rewatch bonus because the whole setup clicks into place on second viewing.

tmdb_overview: George Sr. uses Gob's bachelor party as an opportunity to set up his accountant in a compromising position so that he can blackmail him.  Meanwhile, Michael tries to prove he's a fun guy, and the Funkes reunite their family folk-music band.
insight: Gob's wedding spirals into 53 jokes at 1.95-per-minute velocity, anchored by character chaos and escalating absurdity.
